Academic

        Delhi Public School, Vasant Kunj

    Coursework

    
      Environmental Policy
      Statistical Methods for Policy Analysis
      Comparative Politics of Policy-Making
      Microeconomics I and II: Market Failure & Public Economics
      Management & Implementation in Developing Countries
      Nonprofits, Philanthropy & Policy
      Advanced Regression / Program Evaluation Methods
      Ethics, Values & Public Policy
      Corporate Social Responsibility & Impact Investing
      Global Governance on the Brink
      Global Institutions: UN & Beyond
      Effective Advocacy Communications
      Experimental Design & Implementation
    

    
    Thesis & research

    
      M.P.P. Thesis · Georgetown McCourt School of Public Policy
      The Slaughterhouse Effect: Industrial Livestock Production and Gender-Based Violence Across OECD States
      
        Examines the structural link between the Livestock Production Index and gender-based violence across 38 OECD countries.
        Combines pooled OLS with year fixed effects, synthetic control methods (Netherlands, treatment year 1995), and robustness
        checks using female homicide rates as a time-varying alternative outcome. Surfaces the Reporting Paradox —
        the finding that GBV data is largely time-invariant per country, pointing to systematic underreporting rather than a true
        null effect.
      
      
        Causal inference
        Synthetic control
        Stata
        Gender & development
      
      
        Read the full thesis on ProQuest →
      
    

    
      B.Tech. Thesis · SRM University of Chennai
      AI Model and App for Plant Disease Detection
      
        Developed a mobile application backed by a convolutional neural network (CNN) for real-time detection and classification
        of plant diseases from leaf images. The system achieved high accuracy across multiple crop species, with a field-deployable
        Android app interface designed for low-connectivity agricultural settings. Combined transfer learning with custom training
        data to improve generalization on limited samples.
      
      
        Deep learning · CNN
        Mobile app
        Agricultural tech
        Transfer learning
      
      
        Reproducible code on GitHub →
      
    

    
    Certifications

        iOS Development — Udacity Nanodegree

        Design Thinking
      
      
        🤖

title: Philanthropy Effectiveness Evaluation Tool
text:
Foundation strategy
  Impact evaluation
  Data visualization
  Stata · simulation

title: Philanthropy Effectiveness Evaluation Tool
text:
Where it started
Earlier this semester, I completed a comparative case study of Tides and The Kresge Foundation for my philanthropy class — analyzing how two of the most influential social- and environmental-justice funders in the U.S. deploy capital differently, and why it matters.
That research raised an obvious next question: what if this analysis could be interactive? What if a program officer, a researcher, or a curious student could explore the underlying data themselves, instead of reading it secondhand in a paper?
So I built it.

title: Philanthropy Effectiveness Evaluation Tool
text:
What the dashboard does
A full foundation-strategy dashboard, designed and coded in a day, combining the grant research from the case study with data visualization:

Analyzes sector allocation across NTEE codes (Candid’s nonprofit classification system)
Maps geographic focus and measures grant-size concentration
Scores impact across the OECD DAC’s six evaluation criteria
Produces a grant strategy scorecard benchmarked against industry standards
Tracks an equity lens — BIPOC-led organization prioritization — and its correlation with measurable outcomes
Surfaces actionable recommendations for each foundation based on the above

title: Philanthropy Effectiveness Evaluation Tool
text:
On the data
The dashboard runs on 1,200 simulated grants. The real underlying data lives in the academic case study itself; this build exists to show what a tool like this looks like in practice, not to republish the original research dataset. A companion Stata file documents exactly how the simulated dataset was constructed, so the methodology behind every chart is transparent and reproducible from scratch.

title: Philanthropy Effectiveness Evaluation Tool
text:
Why this, why now
This sits exactly at the intersection I want to keep working in: policy research, program design, and the philanthropic sector — building tools that make evaluation legible to the people who actually have to act on it.
If you work in foundation strategy or impact evaluation, I’d welcome a conversation — including feedback on how to improve the dashboard.

View the repository →

title: Writing Samples — Policy Memos, Research & Essays
text:
To Senator Mark Warner
      Strengthening ESG Investment Standards to Promote Market Transparency
      
        Makes the case for a Federal ESG Disclosure Framework under the SEC, modeled on the EU's Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation — standardized disclosure templates, third-party verification above $500M AUM, and a ban on unverified "green" branding. Walks through four policy options with feasibility, political support, and fiscal cost for each, plus a phased near/medium/long-term implementation timeline.
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  

  
    
      
    
    
      To Shri Ashwini Vaishnaw, Minister for Railways (India)
      Addressing Implementation Barriers in Expansion of Elephant Intrusion Detection Systems (EIDS)
      
        Examines why only 6% of identified elephant-mortality mitigation plans on Indian railway tracks have been implemented, despite an AI monitoring system credited with saving 600+ elephants since 2023. Diagnoses the barriers — slow state-level environmental clearances, no single accountable agency between the Ministry of Railways and MoEFCC, and chronic underfunding relative to railway modernization — and proposes PPP/CSR co-financing to scale the system.
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  

  
    
      
    
    
      Comparative policy research
      Translating International Environmental Agreements into Domestic Policy: India and France Under the Paris Agreement
      
        Compares how India and France translate Paris Agreement NDCs into domestic policy, given their contrasting energy profiles — India's ~70% coal-dependent grid against ambitious renewable targets, France's nuclear-heavy, lower-emission base. Traces the legislative path of India's 500 GW renewable target and France's coal phase-out, and weighs path dependency, public opinion, and political will as drivers of compliance speed in each country.
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  
  
    
      
    
    
      To CARE Foundation & Prof. Kathy Kretman · co-written with Danlei Liao
      Thelma D. Jones Breast Cancer Fund — Grant Proposal Board Memo
      
        Case for a $10,000 grant funding TDJBCF's rebranding, communications, and outreach — reaching breast cancer survivors in Washington D.C.'s Ward 6, where Black women face a mortality rate twice that of white women. Lays out a budget, an impact-measurement framework across four metric categories, and the organization's reach (~72,000 people across DC, Northern Virginia, and Maryland).
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  
  
    
      
    
    
      To G20 Finance Ministers & the IMF Executive Board
      Global Financial Institutional Adaptation to Climate Vulnerability and Sovereign Debt
      
        Argues that the Bretton Woods debt architecture was built for episodic liquidity crises, not the cumulative, structural climate shocks now facing low-emission, high-vulnerability states. Proposes four reforms — climate-resilient debt clauses, scaled debt-for-climate swaps, a reformed G20 Common Framework, and a new Climate Vulnerability Adjustment Mechanism for debt sustainability analysis.
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  

  
    
      
    
    
      Persona writing exercise — voiced as G-24 Secretariat Director, to the V20 Chair
      Scaling Debt-for-Nature Swaps Through a V20 Sovereign Mechanism
      
        A coursework exercise written in the voice of a real institutional role, proposing a sovereign-backed V20 Nature Insurance Pool to underwrite biodiversity-linked debt and scale debt-for-nature swaps beyond one-off bilateral deals like Belize's and Seychelles'. Includes a full annex set: comparative case studies, a cost-benefit analysis, and a risk-assessment matrix.
      
      Download PDF ↗
    
  

  
    
      
    
    
      Academic essay · Georgetown McCourt School
      The Invisible Costs of AI: Global Data Labour and the Limits of Ethical Governance
      
        Argues that AI governance frameworks externalize psychological and economic harm onto Global South data labelers — workers like Fasica in Nairobi, paid roughly $1.50/hour to review graphic content — while concentrating profit and decision-making in the Global North. Draws on distributive and cosmopolitan justice (Rawls, Pogge) and social contract theory to argue current "consent" framing doesn't hold, and proposes treating content moderation as high-risk labour with mandatory protections, data-labour royalties, and Global South representation in AI governance bodies.
      
      Download PDF ↗

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
text:
Fuzzy matching
  ArcGIS · spmap
  Causal inference
  Choropleth mapping

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)
text:
Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)
Tanzania’s ward count grew from 3,333 to 3,944 between 2010 and 2015 through administrative subdivision. Matching electoral datasets across this boundary change required reconciling two non-overlapping geographic classifications — a standard problem in any longitudinal sub-national policy analysis.
Method: Used ArcGIS shapefile intersection data (2012 and 2017 shapefiles) to compute the percentage area overlap between each 2015 ward and all candidate 2010 parent wards. Applied a 50% area-overlap threshold to classify parent-child relationships.

  
    2,705
    Stable wards (unchanged 2010–2015)
  
  
    488
    Split into exactly 2 new wards
  
  
    45
    Split into 3 or more wards
  
  
    440 / 616
    Parentless / orphan wards
  

Top regions by ward division rate:



Region
Division rate
Context




Rukwa
39.7%
Highest — major administrative restructuring


Morogoro
34.7%



Katavi
28.6%



Mtwara
28.5%



Arusha
25.6%



Kagera
5.0%
Lowest — minimal redistricting



Policy relevance: This kind of boundary reconciliation is essential for any longitudinal analysis of sub-national government data — elections, health outcomes, census linkages, or fiscal transfers.

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation
text:
Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation
Designed a DGP with a known treatment effect (β = 0.3 SD), then built 5 regression models varying which controls were included — confounder, mediator, and collider — running 200 simulations at each of 6 sample sizes.
Key finding: Only Model 2 (controlling for the confounder alone) recovers β = 0.30. A larger sample size cannot fix structural bias — Models 1 and 5 remain wrong at N=2,500.



Model
Controls included
Bias direction
Recovers β = 0.30?




M1: Naive
None
Upward (~0.57)
❌


M2: Correct
Confounder only
None
✅


M3: + Mediator
Confounder + mediator
None (higher variance)
✅


M4: Collider
Confounder + collider
Downward (~0.15)
❌


M5: All
All covariates
Fully attenuated (~0.04)
❌



Collider warning: Including a variable caused by both treatment and outcome introduces downward bias of ~0.15 — a counterintuitive and commonly overlooked error in applied policy research that gets worse with more data.

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth
text:
Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth
Merged a hand-coded Animal Welfare Penalty Harshness Index (0–100 scale, 48 states) with U.S. Census shapefiles in Stata using spmap, producing a choropleth revealing stark regional disparities in enforcement.
Method: shp2dta to import Census shapefiles → merge with penalty index → spmap with quantile color breaks → explicit handling of missing data (WY, SD) shown in legend as gray.
Finding: Northeast and Pacific Coast states score 75+; the Deep South and Mountain West cluster below 35 — consistent with broader regulatory divergence patterns in U.S. federalism.

  Skills demonstrated
  
    Geospatial fuzzy matching
    Shapefile processing
    Panel data reconciliation
    Stata bysort / merge
    DAG reasoning
    Collider / confounder identification
    Choropleth mapping
    ArcGIS · spmap

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Stata · simulate
  Monte Carlo methods
  Power analysis
  Cluster RCT design

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
section: Part 1: Sampling noise in a fixed population
text:
Part 1: Sampling noise in a fixed population
Generated a fixed population of 10,000 individuals with a true Y–X relationship (seed-fixed for reproducibility), then ran 500 regression simulations at four sample sizes to quantify how estimation precision changes as we draw larger subsamples from a bounded population.
Key finding: At N=10, beta estimates vary wildly across simulations despite being unbiased on average. At N=10,000 (the full population), all sampling variance disappears entirely — this is the finite-population ceiling.



Sample size (N)
Mean β̂
Avg. SEM
95% CI width
% significant




10
≈ true β
High
Very wide
~30–40%


100
≈ true β
Moderate
Wide
~70–80%


1,000
≈ true β
Small
Narrow
~98%+


10,000
= true β
Near zero
Minimal
100%

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
section: Part 2: Sampling noise in an infinite superpopulation
text:
Part 2: Sampling noise in an infinite superpopulation
Extended the simulation to an infinite superpopulation DGP — generating fresh data each run — across 26 sample sizes: all 20 powers of 2 (4 → 524,288) plus N = 10, 100, 1,000, 10,000, 100,000, and 1,000,000.
Why superpopulation allows N = 1,000,000: In Part 1 the population is capped at 10,000. The superpopulation DGP draws from an infinite distribution each run — sample size is unbounded.
SEM difference: In the superpopulation, variance shrinks as 1/√N but never hits zero. At the same N, superpopulation SEM is slightly larger than fixed-population SEM, because the fixed population’s known finite structure removes a layer of uncertainty.

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Part 3: Individual-level power calculations
Designed a DGP where Y ~ N(0,1), treatment effect = 0.1 SD (uniform 0.0–0.2 SD), 50/50 treatment-control split. Calculated minimum sample sizes for 80% power under three scenarios:

  
    ~3,142
    Baseline N (80% power, 0.1 SD effect)
  
  
    ~3,697
    After 15% attrition (+18% larger)
  
  
    ~4,190
    30% treatment arm (unequal split)
  

Attrition: Losing 15% of participants symmetrically inflates required sample by ~18% — you must recruit enough to still have sufficient power after dropout.
Unequal arms: Shifting to 30/70 treatment-control requires a larger total N. The smaller arm is the binding constraint on precision. The 50/50 split is always the most efficient design for a fixed total N.

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Part 4: Cluster RCT design — ICC = 0.3
Designed a school-based intervention where treatment is assigned at the school level — not student level. Treatment effect = 0.2 SD (uniform 0.15–0.25 SD), ICC/ρ ≈ 0.3.
Design effect at 15 students/school: DEFF = 1 + (15–1) × 0.3 = 5.2 — meaning you need roughly 5× as many observations as an individual-level RCT.
Power plateaus quickly: With 200 schools fixed, power reaches ~80% around 8–16 students/school. Beyond that, adding students yields near-zero gains due to high ICC.








Scenario
Schools needed
Total students




Full compliance (100% uptake)
~100 schools
~1,500


70% adoption (partial compliance)
~145 schools
~2,175



Partial compliance finding: When only 70% of assigned schools implement the intervention, the required school count rises by ~45%. Non-compliance is a design problem, not just a fidelity problem.

  Skills demonstrated
  
    Monte Carlo simulation
    Stata simulate command
    RCT design
    Power analysis
    Cluster randomization
    ICC / design effect
    Attrition modeling
    Reproducible code
